subject: History
chapter: Chapter: Weavers, Iron Smelters and Factory Owners

key_points: Before British rule, Indian textiles were famous worldwide and produced by skilled weavers using traditional techniques
Indian textiles such as muslin, silk and cotton were exported to Europe, Africa and Southeast Asia
With the Industrial Revolution in Britain, machine-made textiles were produced in large quantities
British policies encouraged the import of British machine-made cloth into India and discouraged Indian textile production
Indian weavers faced severe competition from cheaper British factory-made textiles
Many weavers lost their livelihoods due to declining demand for handwoven cloth
Iron smelters in India used traditional furnaces to produce iron, but their work declined due to colonial forest laws and the introduction of modern iron production
The rise of factories and industrial production changed traditional occupations and economic structures in India
examples: Indian muslin cloth produced in Bengal that was famous for its fine quality
British textile mills in Manchester producing machine-made cloth
Traditional iron smelting carried out by tribal communities using charcoal furnaces
Decline of Indian weaving communities due to imported British textiles
formulas: Decline of Indian weaving → British industrialization + Machine-made imports + Colonial trade policies
Industrial change → Traditional crafts ↓ + Factory production ↑
Impact of colonial rule → Economic exploitation + Loss of traditional industries
exam_tips: Explain how British industrialization affected Indian weavers
Mention the impact of colonial policies on traditional industries
Give examples such as muslin production and Manchester textiles
Explain the changes in iron production and the decline of traditional smelting
